intro:
A covenant (Hebrew berîth; Greek diathēkē) is a solemn, binding relationship between parties with sworn promises and obligations. It is the central organizing category of biblical theology: God’s chosen way of binding himself to a people in order to redeem them, so that the storyline of Scripture advances through a succession of covenants culminating in Christ.

The Old Testament unfolds a series of divine covenants, each marked by initiative, promise, and a sign. With Noah, God pledged never again to destroy the earth by flood, sealed by the rainbow (Genesis 9:8–17). With Abraham, he promised offspring, land, and blessing to all nations (Genesis 12:1–3; Genesis 15:18; Genesis 17:7), signed by circumcision. At Sinai, through Moses, he formed Israel as his treasured people under the law, with blessing for obedience and curse for rebellion (Exodus 19:5–6; Deuteronomy 7:9). To David he promised an everlasting throne and a son whose kingdom would never end (2 Samuel 7:12–16).

Israel broke the Mosaic covenant, and the prophets foretold a new covenant: God would write his law on the heart, forgive sin fully, and give his Spirit (Jeremiah 31:31–34; Ezekiel 36:26–27). Jesus inaugurated it at the Last Supper — "This cup is the new covenant in my blood" (Luke 22:20; 1 Corinthians 11:25). Hebrews presents him as "the mediator of a better covenant" (Hebrews 8:6) and, playing on diathēkē as "will/testament," shows that the covenant takes effect through the death of the testator (Hebrews 9:15).

Covenant thus binds the whole Bible into one story: the one faithful God progressively committing himself to save a people, every promise finding its "Yes" in Christ (2 Corinthians 1:20). All who are in Christ are members of his new-covenant people, heirs of the promise made to Abraham (Galatians 3:29).
